data.title: Techtronic Industries: Consider Earnings Headwinds And Share Gain Potential (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My view of Techtronic Industries Company Limited (OTCQX:TTNDY) (OTCQX:TTNDF) (0669.HK) or "TICL" has become Neutral.

I previewed the company's 2H24 performance in the prior January 16, 2025 write-up.

A Buy for TICL is no longer justified. The stock's FY25 prospects will be impacted by "Liberation Day tariffs", taking into account its geographical exposure.

But I'm not bearish on this name. TTNDY could grab a bigger slice of the pie or the industry it's operating in. There are substantial differences in scale and sourcing mix between the company and certain peers.

Major Competitor's Latest Disclosures Have Negative Read-throughs For TTNDF's Near-Term Outlook

TICL reports its financials on a semi-annual frequency. Its 1H25 numbers will only be released in August.

You would have to analyze Stanley Black & Decker's (SWK) or "SBD's" recent numbers and commentary to understand how tariffs will affect TTNDY.

TTNDF and SWK are the top and second largest players in the U.S. power tools sector boasting market shares of 25% and 21%, respectively. I obtained these figures from Barclays' (BCS) April 10, 2025 report ("not publicly accessible") titled "Lowering SWK Estimates".

SBD cut its FY25 per-share bottomline guide from $5.25 to $4.50, when the company announced its first quarter results in end-April.

SWK is anticipating additional tariff-related costs of roughy $1,700M for the current year assuming the status quo.

SG&A ("Selling, General & Administrative") expense optimization and ASP ("Average Selling Price") increases are counter-measures SBD will take.

But the group will most probably suffer from volume contraction, as price elasticity leads to lower demand. It also has to undertake supply chain investments required for production shifts to minimize tariffs.

The management guidance referenced above points to a mid-teens percentage hit to SWK's earnings factoring in the puts and takes.

It's worth noting that both entities are heavily dependent on the American market.

TTNDY derived three-quarters of its FY2024 topline from the NA ("North America") segment. In the same time period, the United States represented 62% of SBD's revenue.

For SWK, around "60%" of its domestic COGS is attributable to US/Mexico, while another 15% is linked to China. Its Chinese manufacturing exposure is fairly modest, so there shouldn't be issues pivoting away from this high-tariff ("over 100% currently") nation over time.

In the case of TTNDF, the company has 30%, 40%, and 30% ("source: S&P Capital IQ") of its production capacity in China, Vietnam, Mexico/U.S., respectively. TICL should be able to make more of its products in either America or a low-tariff ("10% as it stands now") region like the Vietnamese market in due course.

My prediction is that TTNDY's FY2025 net income will be impacted by a high-teens percent because of the trade war. This is more severe than that for SBD. I have considered Techtronic's higher PRC ("People's Republic of China") sourcing contribution and the lower proportion of goods made directly in the US. I'm forecasting that TTNDF's EBITDA growth will slow from +16% in FY24 to +10% this year.

But TICL's Market Share Might Grow At The Expense Of Smaller Rivals

The tariff crisis could turn into an opportunity for leaders such as Techtronic to extend their dominance.

As per BCS's research that I referred to above, TICL and SBD are two to three times as large as their peers.

I can use Japan's Makita (OTCPK:MKTAY) (OTCPK:MKEWF) as an example.

MKEWF's US power tool market share is just 8%, or 17 ppts ("percentage points") below that of TTNDF.

TTNDY's TTM ("Trailing Twelve Months") group-level sales are about 3x MKTAY's in the same time frame.

The actual FY18-24 topline CAGRs for Techtronic and Makita were +13.0% and +7.6%, respectively. This disparity in historical performance implies that the former had gained share in the past couple of years.

TTNDF is likely to have capitalized on the size advantage to spread its non-variable expenses over a wider revenue base. The resulting operating leverage should allow TICL to achieve higher margins and invest more in R&D ("Research & Development") to widen its competitive edge.

I'm of the opinion that the ongoing trade war will benefit the bigger players due to scale economies. Sub-scale operators will have their margins squeezed further by tariffs, making them even more uncompetitive against giants like Techtronic that have an edge in scale.

Separately, TICL's Chinese competitors are most probably going to be share donors in the foreseeable future.

Hangzhou Greatstar ("HG") (002444.SZ) and Chervon Holdings ("CH") (2285.HK) are TTNDY's other rivals one should watch. Both of them generate around 60-70% of their respective revenues from the US according to S&P Capital IQ data.

But CH and HG are companies from China with manufacturing concentrated in their home market ("China") which has been penalized with tariffs surpassing 100%. They will find it tough to go head-to-head with TTNDF which has production sites in lower-tariff ("10% now") Vietnam and the zero-tariff U.S.

My Closing Remarks

I'm rating Techtronic as a Hold now, as opposed to a Buy previously.

TTNDF's profit will be hurt by the new US tariffs, and its EBITDA expansion will moderate in FY25 as per my estimates. This explains why I am not positive on the stock anymore.

But a Sell for TTNDY will be unwarranted. The company has share gain potential, in view of its non-China production footprint and leading position in the power tools space.

TICL's consensus forward EV/EBITDA is 10.3 times ("source: S&P Capital IQ"). This is quite close to SBD's 9.9x EBITDA multiple.

Also, a valuation rule of thumb is that a stock's earnings multiple should be on par with its income growth. My projection of a +10% FY25 EBITDA increase is approximately at the same level as its EV-EBITDA ratio ("10.3x").

I think that TTNDF is reasonably valued, which supports my new ("Hold") rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bio-Rad: With Low Debt And A Stock Price Drop, Is The Company Undervalued?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I rate Bio-Rad (NYSE:BIO) with a buy rating. I start by mentioning that the low growth perspective is the negative aspect. For 2025, the company expects a 0.25% growth rate for its revenues, which might not sound promising for some investors. However, since I published my previous article on Bio-Rad in November 2024, the company's share price has declined by 30.81%. Due to this drop, the two discounted cash flow models implemented in this article suggest that Bio-Rad's share price will increase. The first discounted cash flow (DCF) model points out an undervaluation of 33.91%, while the second valuation indicates a 0.53% undervaluation.

In my view, the low debt reported by the company is another positive aspect. Bio-Rad has a 20.50% debt-to-equity ratio, which is relatively low. In addition, it has a 5.99 current ratio. These figures indicate that the company can endure difficult financial situations. Another negative aspect is the current trade war between the U.S. and several countries. Asian and European nations could retaliate against Bio-Rad for being an American company. Pondering the negative and positive aspects, I consider that Bio-Rad is a good investment opportunity.

Introduction

In November 2024, I wrote an article about Bio-Rad, indicating that the company's fair value was $259 per share, suggesting a 20.10% overvaluation. The current share price of Bio-Rad is $232.55, which is below the fair value estimated in my previous article.

The financial statements explain why Bio-Rad's stock price dropped. In 2024, the company's revenues declined by 3.92% annually, while its income from operations fell by 20.37%. When comparing 2024Q4 with 2023Q3, the revenues declined by 2.01%. These results indicate that the company had a challenging year.

In the first quarter of 2025, Bio-Rad also saw disappointing results. The revenues declined by 4.2% year-on-year in that quarter. Bio-Rad indicated that its Life Sciences segment has experienced headwinds in the academic research market. In addition, the company said that its sales in the Clinical Diagnostics segment were affected by lower reimbursement for diagnostic testing in China.

Given the recent events, I think it is convenient to reanalyze the company. Additionally, I recently wrote an article about Sartorius AG, in which I estimated the fair value of that company; Bio-Rad has a significant investment in Sartorius AG. With the fair values estimated from my Sartorius AG article, I use those values as input in this article.

Understanding the business

Bio-Rad is one of those rare cases in which its profits came from a non-core business, as depicted in the following chart. For instance, in 2024, the company reported a net income of $269 million, while it registered losses of $2,656.8 million in its position of equity securities and loans receivable. Due to these losses, Bio-Rad reported a net loss of $1,844.2 million.

Business Segments

The company has two business segments, namely Life Sciences and Clinical Diagnostics. In 2024, the Life Sciences segment accounted for 40.07% of Bio-Rad's net sales in the core business, while the Clinical Diagnostics segment represented the remaining 59.93%. These two segments do not include Bio-Rad's investment in Sartorius AG.

COVID-19 has affected the Life Sciences segment's net sales. Since 2021, the revenues of this segment have experienced a sharp decline, as depicted in the following chart. Governments and institutions have reduced their acquisition of COVID-19-related products and services. Consequently, the products that Bio-Rad developed and produced to mitigate the pandemic have seen their demand decline. In addition, COVID-19 has brought another negative effect, which is the elevated inventories that companies accumulated during the pandemic. Biopharmaceutical companies have used their inventories and have reduced the acquisition of new products and services. The 2024Q3 Earnings Transcripts of Bio-Rad reveal this situation.

In the Life Sciences segment, Bio-Rad offers products in several categories. The most relevant are PCR & qPCR, chromatography, digital PCR, cell analysis, etc. In the 2024Q4 Earnings Transcripts, the company reveals that the sales of chromatography products declined by approximately 50%. In the transcripts, it also mentions that it expects a decline of 1.5% ((mid-point)) for the Life Science segment this year. In 2025, Bio-Rad expects to acquire Stilla Technologies for $225 million. In my view, the company still faces headwinds in the Life Sciences segment.

On the other hand, the Clinical Diagnostic segment shows better figures, as depicted in the following chart. In the last year, its net sales increased by 3.26%. Meanwhile, for 2025, the company expects a 0.5%-2.5% growth outlook in the segment.

Overall, Bio-Rad will wade through a challenging year in 2025, with a small growth perspective for its revenues. Due to this pessimistic outlook, the market has reacted negatively, which has led to a decline in the company's stock price.

Sartorius AG

Bio-Rad is a significant investor in Sartorius AG. In fact, most of the losses registered due to a change in its investment in equity securities and loan receivable came from its investment in Sartorius AG. As of December 2024, Bio-Rad held 38% of the ordinary common shares and 28% of the ordinary preferred shares. To grant context, 51.68% of Bio-Rad's assets are other investments. Consequently, Bio-Rad's stock performance is correlated to Sartorius AG's stock performance.

I advise the reader to check my article about Sartorius AG, written on March 31, 2025. The article explains that in 2028, the community of heirs will receive full ownership of its inheritance. This event could be the spark for Bio-Rad to try to control that company, which could modify Bio-Rad's investment decision for the upcoming years.

Valuation

In my previous article about Bio-Rad, I valued the company using the DCF model. Consequently, I think it is time to update the model and reestimate the fair value of the company. The procedure is to value Bio-Rad's core business, which consists of the Life Sciences and Diagnostics segments. Subsequently, I used Sartorius AG's fair value estimated in my recent article of that company.

In this year, the company expects growth in its revenues in the range of negative 1% to 1.5%. Meanwhile, between 2026 and 2029, I project the revenues using the compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of Bio-Rad's revenues from 2017 and 2024, which is 2.49%. In the last seven years, the company has not exhibited an elevated growth rate, as depicted in the following table. Consequently, I think that 2.49% is a reasonable assumption.

Moving to the operating income margin, Bio-Rad expects an 11% operating income margin for 2025. Meanwhile, for 2026 to 2029, I use the average of the operating income margin using the years 2017, 2019, 2023, and 2024, as depicted in the chart above. The average is 9.65%. COVID-19 was the catalyst for the company to obtain a higher operating income margin between 2020 and 2022. Consequently, I decided not to include those years, with the scope of being conservative with my valuation.

The tax rate to use will be 23.82%. This value is the average of the tax rate between 2018 and 2021. During 2022-2024, the net incomes were negative, as observed in the following chart; for this reason, these years were not included to estimate the tax rate for the upcoming years.

The following chart shows Bio-Rad's depreciation and amortization ((D&A)) between 2017 and 2024. In my opinion, the average of the last five years, which is $141.7 million, is a reasonable approximation.

The last two items extracted from the financial statements to project are capital expenditure ((capex)) and change in working capital. I forecast them as a proportion of the revenues. For instance, between 2020 and 2024, the average of the ratio capex-to-revenues was 4.96%. With this percentage, I projected Bio-Rad's capex for 2025-2029. Meanwhile, on average, the ratio change in working capital-to-revenues was 2.07% between 2020 and 2024. With these averages, I projected Bio-Rad's change in working capital for the period 2025-2029.

In this article, I estimate the perpetual growth rate using an average between the 10-year U.S. government bond yield and its German counterparty. In 2024, the United States represented 40.58% of Bio-Rad's revenues, while Europe accounted for 32.50% of the revenues. Consequently, using only the 10-year U.S. government bond yield to estimate the perpetual growth rate will not take into consideration Bio-Rad's international operation. Therefore, I use the average between the German and American 10-year government bond yields. For this purpose, I estimated the average of both yields between March 1, 2015, and March 1, 2025, as depicted in the following table. The average is 1.59%. I use this rate as the perpetual growth rate. Finally, I took the weighted average cost of capital (OTC:WACC), which is 8.56%, from the Alpha Spread website.

With the previous elements discussed in this section, I implemented the DCF model, yielding that the fair value of only Bio-Rad's Life Sciences and Clinical Diagnostics is $121.60 per share, as depicted in the following tables.

It is necessary to value Bio-Rad's other investments to have a full valuation of the company. As mentioned in this article, Bio-Rad has a considerable investment in Sartorius AG. In 2024, Bio-Rad reported in the balance sheet other investments for $4,839.2 million. From this value, Bio-Rad indicated that $4,469.20 million corresponds to its investment in Sartorius AG ((at market value)). The remaining $370 million is investments in other financial instruments. As such, it is crucial to value Sartorius AG to reflect this investment.

I recently wrote an article about Sartorius AG, in which I implemented two DCF models. I use that article as valuable input to finish the valuation of Bio-Rad. It is a recent article, and I think the estimation implemented depicts the current reality of Sartorius AG. The first model uses a ten-year window to forecast Sartorius AG's free cash flow, yielding that the equity value of that company is €10,998 million. Meanwhile, the second model uses a five-year window to project Sartorius AG's free cash flow. The equity value of Sartorius AG obtained with that model is €6,178 million. These are the inputs necessary to finish Bio-Rad's valuation. Bio-Rad has 32.70% of Sartorius AG's outstanding shares. Consequently, with this percentage, I estimated the equity value of Sartorius that belongs to Bio-Rad, as depicted in the following table.

With Bio-Rad's stake in Sartorius AG, I calculated the fair value of Bio-Rad, as depicted in the following table. My estimation suggests that Bio-Rad is undervalued by 33.91% when using a ten-year horizon to value Sartorius AG. Meanwhile, the pessimistic valuation, which uses five years to forecast Sartorius AG's free cash flow, suggests that the company presents an overvaluation of 0.53%.

Risk

The recent shifts in the trade policy that the U.S. has conducted could have negative effects on Bio-Rad. The following chart shows that more than half of Bio-Rad's revenues did not originate in the U.S. in 2024. As such, countries could retaliate against Bio-Rad for being an American company. For instance, I recently wrote an article in which I indicated that China's Ministry of Commerce placed Illumina (ILMN), an American company, on the backlist. It prohibited Illumina from selling DNA sequencing machines to the Asian country. Illumina is a Life Sciences Tools company, as is Bio-Rad. The U.S. responded by placing more than 50 Chinese companies on its blacklist. This situation could escalate and Bio-Rad could finish in the Chinese blacklist.

Peer Analysis

This section implements a peer analysis among Bio-Rad, Becton, Dixon and Company (BDX), Danaher (DHR), Thermo Fisher (TMO), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), Abbott Laboratories (ABT), BioMerieux (OTCPK:BMXMF), Tosoh (TOSOH), and Merck & Co (MRK), as shown in the following chart. In the 2023 Annual Report, Bio-Rad mentions that these companies are its main competitors.

For 2025, Bio-Rad expects P/E and EV/EBITDA above the median value in the peer group, suggesting that the company is overvalued among its peers. However, it has lower price-to-book and price-to-cash flow ratios than its peers, indicating that Bio-Rad is undervalued among its peers. As such, the valuation metrics report mixed results.

The company has seen a significant drop in its stock price. For instance, in the last year, Bio-Rad experienced a 16.92% decline in its stock price. In addition, the firm exhibits the largest stock price drop in five years.

Moving to the growth ratios, Bio-Rad expects headwinds for 2025. Its revenues are expected to decline by 0.70%, while the company expects a 5.28% fall in the EBITDA. These figures are the least appealing values among its peers. Additionally, the company saw a 17.11% CAGR in its EBITDA in the last three years.

On the other hand, Bio-Rad projected a gross profit margin slightly lower than the median in the peer group. Furthermore, it reported a negative return on equity of 27.51%. Consequently, this last result suggests that the company experienced a challenging year.

Finally, the debt ratios show positive results for Bio-Rad. The company has the second lowest debt-to-equity ratio. In addition, it has a 5.99 current ratio, suggesting that it can pay its short-term debt with minor problems.

Overall, the company exhibits issues in the revenue and EBITDA growth metrics. For this year, Bio-Rad expects a decline in its EBITDA. Additionally, its stock price sank by 49.49%, the largest drop in the last five years among the peer group. The positive aspect is its relatively low debt levels. To grant context, the 20.50% debt-to-equity ratio indicates that the company can resist difficult situations.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I rate Bio-Rad with a buy rating. Although the company will face problems expanding its operations, it will continue to grow, which is a positive aspect. For instance, in 2025, the firm expects a 0.25% increase in its revenues. By segment levels, Bio-Rad projected that its Life Sciences segment will grow by 1.5% this year, while the Clinical Diagnostics segment is projected to grow by only 0.5% to 2.5%.

In my view, the share price decline that the company has experienced since Seeking Alpha published my previous article on Bio-Rad has occasioned an interesting investment opportunity. Due to the stock price drop of 30.81%, the two discounted cash flow models suggest that Bio-Rad's stock price will increase. For example, the DCF model that I implemented using a horizon of ten years to forecast the FCFs suggests there is an undervaluation of 33.91%. Meanwhile, the DCF model that uses a five-year horizon to forecast the FCFs indicates that the company is undervalued by 0.53%. The company's low debt levels are another positive aspect. Bio-Rad has a 20.50% debt-to-equity ratio, which suggests that the company has financial resources to endure difficult financial situations. Having considered all these aspects, I rate Bio-Rad with a buy rating.

data.title: Gold Market Commentary: A Risk-Induced Premium That May Linger
data.description:
Dollar dive and vol spike drove gold up

Gold continued its ascent in April, breaking the US$3,500/oz mark in intra-day trading during the month.1 While gold pulled back from its record highs, it still finished strong, above US$3,300/oz and rising by 6% m/m (Table 1). Gold’s return was more modest in developed market currencies and even fell slightly in Swiss francs on the back of local currency strength versus the dollar.

In fact, our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) points to the significant plunge in the US dollar – captured by ‘opportunity cost (FX)’ – as one of the key drivers of gold’s performance in April (Chart 1). Other contributing factors were a spike in market volatility and geopolitical concerns (‘risk and uncertainty’). The model also suggests that there was a degree of mean reversion that created a drag on gold’s performance, as some investors likely took profits following four consecutive months of strong returns (‘momentum’).

Chart 1: A combination of USD weakness, market volatility and rising geopolitical risk helped gold move higher

Key drivers of gold’s return by month*

*Data to 30 April 2025. Our Gold Return Attribution Model (OTCPK:GRAM) is a multiple regression model of monthly gold price returns, which we group into four key thematic driver categories of gold’s performance: economic expansion, risk & uncertainty, opportunity cost, and momentum. These themes capture motives behind gold demand; most importantly, investment demand, which is considered the marginal driver of gold price returns in the short run. The ‘residual’ represents the percentage change in the gold price that is not explained by factors already included. Results shown here are based on analysis covering a five-year estimation period using monthly data. Alternative estimation periods and data frequencies are available on Goldhub.com.

Table 1: Gold continued its upward run in almost all currencies amidst USD weakness vs. developed market currencies

Gold price and performance in key currencies*

USD(oz) EUR(oz) JPY(g) GBP(oz) CAD(oz) CHF(oz) INR(10g) RMB(g) TRY(oz) AUD(oz)
April price* 3,302 2,915 15,189 2,477 4,556 2,727 93,928 779 127,107 5,158
April return* 6.0% 1.2% 1.1% 2.7% 1.7% -1.0% 5.9% 6.9% 7.5% 3.4%
Y-t-d return* 26.6% 15.7% 15.2% 18.8% 21.4% 15.2% 23.7% 26.4% 37.8% 22.3%
Record high price* 3,434 3,006 15,628 2,575 4,743 2,812 98,228 830 131,387 5,393
Record high date* 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025 22-Apr2025
Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council*As of 30 April 2025. Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD, expressed in local currencies, except for India and China where the MCX Gold Price PM and Shanghai Gold Benchmark PM are used, respectively.

Can gold’s run last?

Gold is up by nearly 27% y-t-d, significantly outperforming major asset classes.2 Not surprisingly, investors are asking what’s behind the move and how sustainable it might be.

Gold has been supported by a combination of:

  • US trade policy uncertainty and, more generally, geoeconomic risk
  • A weakening US dollar
  • Higher inflation expectations combined with lower bond yield
  • Continued central bank demand.

Against this backdrop, investment flows via gold ETFs have significantly ramped up. In Q1, gold ETFs amassed US$21bn of inflows – the strongest quarter in three years – with an additional US$11bn in April. Collectively, US funds have led the way, but Chinese funds have increased their holdings by a whopping 77% y-t-d.

Early innings?

Does this mean that the gold investment market is becoming saturated? We don’t believe that’s the case.

Previous gold bull runs have coincided with significant inflows in gold ETFs. But there seems to be room to grow. For example, gold holdings by funds listed in Western markets are 575t (or 15%) below their record high.3 In addition, Asian gold ETFs, driven by Chinese and Indian investors, have been consistently growing for the past two years, signalling a structural shift in adoption.

Furthermore, COMEX futures net longs, which typically represent the more speculative end of the investment spectrum, do not look overextended. They are currently sitting near 570t – their lowest level in more than a year and well below their 2020 high of over 1,200t.

This, of course, would not rule out further profit taking by some market participants and potential pullbacks in price.

Risk by any other name…is still risk

Investors have grown increasingly concerned over the growth and inflation outlook from the fallout of the ongoing trade war, both in the US and globally (Chart 2). The rise in uncertainty around trade policy and international relations has been supportive of gold as investors typically turn towards safe-haven assets for downside protection in those types of environments.

Chart 2: Trade policy uncertainty is extremely elevated

This has been exacerbated by pressure on US Treasuries and the dollar, which traditionally function as safe havens. This phenomenon is well documented by the media.4 In addition, conversations with wealth managers suggest that, for the first time in a long time, many investors have been seeking to hedge their overexposure to US dollar assets.

We estimate that trade concerns have accounted for approximately 10% to 15% of gold’s return y-t-d, stemming from USD devaluation, heightened geopolitical and market risk, and at least partly from some of the investment flows we’ve seen in recent weeks.5

However, even if trade negotiations were to progress and conditions to improve, we would not expect gold to completely reverse its risk-induced bump.

For one, gold remains well bid despite some easing of trade tensions and the noteworthy rebound in the US stock market since early April. In addition, investors – especially international ones – appear wary of policies on which the Trump administration may concentrate next…and all other policies that may come over the following three and a half years.

Focusing on the ‘real’ side of real rates

A major concern regarding US trade policies is the potential effect they could have on US and global inflation. Indeed, short-term inflation is expected to rise in the US according to consumers and market measures (Chart 3).

Chart 3: US inflation projections are on the rise

Generally, high inflation is supportive for gold as investors seek out real assets for protection amidst falling purchasing power. Inflation, however, is often accompanied by higher rates that may create a drag on performance.

In this instance, there may be a limit to how much interest rates may rise:

The Fed has become a little more dovish recently. According to the Fedspeak Index, the FOMC is now very much on the fence as it balances the need to control inflation with supporting slowing growth (Chart 4).

Chart 4: The Fed is sitting on the fence, as it balances addressing potentially high inflation with slower growth

Fed funds target and Fedspeak Index*

At the same time, bond market participants certainly think that the Fed will prioritise economic health in this stagflationary-led dilemma (Chart 5).

Chart 5: Bond markets are pricing in more rate cuts

Even if the Fed were to turn more hawkish, which we believe would only occur in the event of longer-lasting inflation effects, gold could remain supported.

Using GRAM, we have analysed the effect that changes in inflation and yields can have on gold, holding other variables constant. The main conclusion is that, in this environment, a rise in inflation will likely have a more positive effect on gold’s performance than the potential drag that higher rates may bring (Table 2).

Table 2: The positive effect of rising inflation on gold in the current environment may overcome a possible drag from interest rates

Hypothetical effect on gold’s return from changes in inflation and interest rates holding other drivers constant*

Source: Bloomberg, World Gold Council*As of 30 April 2025. Based on the estimation of sensitivities of explanatory variables included in GRAM, and the hypothetical effect that a change in inflation and yields may have, by holding other variables constant. We use 2-year yields and breakevens instead of 10-year ones, which we normally use in GRAM, to account for the more immediate effect from tariffs. Results shown here are based on analysis covering a five-year estimation period using monthly data.

Looking beyond investors

We have covered multiple reasons why gold investment may remain strong. However, it is important to consider potential headwinds.

While investment flows are the key driver of large gold price movements, consumers are an important contributor to gold’s performance in the medium and long term. And they are key to sustaining gold trends. Higher gold prices have been deterring some jewellery buyers and while consumers can adjust to higher price levels, they still need time to adapt.

At present, recycling has remained surprisingly muted, but deteriorating economic conditions could change this, bringing additional supply and adding pressure to gold.

Central banks have also been an important source of demand for the past three years, significantly contributing to gold’s performance. We still expect central bank demand to remain robust this year, but rapidly rising prices have, in the past, temporarily decelerated purchases.

In sum…

Gold’s performance so far this year has been remarkable. But the speed at which it has occurred has raised concerns about its endurance.

We believe that structural reasons will enable investment demand to continue to thrive:

  • Uncertainty surrounding US policies and their effect on the dollar
  • More sensitivity to higher inflation expectations and a higher likelihood of lower interest rates
  • Lower gold accumulation levels than in previous cycles.

That, of course, would not prevent potential pullbacks driven by profit taking or signs of advancements in trade negotiations.

Equally, for gold’s bull run to be sustainable for longer, consumers need to be given time to adapt to higher prices.

Footnotes

1Based on gold’s spot price (XAU) as of 22 April 2025.

2Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM as of 30 April 2025.

3Collective gold holdings across all listed gold ETFs in North America and Europe reached a record high of 3,746t on 6 November 2020; their holdings as of 30 April 2025 are 3,170t.

4US Treasuries sell off as trade war calls haven status into question, Morningstar, 9 April 2025.

5As of 30 April 2025. Estimated by adding contributions from the US dollar, GPR, market volatility and gold ETF flows from GRAM over the past two months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Zoetis' Q1: Market Overreaction Or Justified Drop?
data.description:
Introduction

Zoetis Inc. (NYSE:ZTS) reported Q1 earnings on Tuesday. The market did not like them, and the stock dropped more than 5% that day. It did, however, recover the entirety of this drop between Wednesday and Thursday:

I received many questions asking if the numbers warranted such a drop. While it’s always impossible to know why stocks drop or rise any given day (I recommend reading "Flash Crash" to understand why), I think there was a palpable reason for this drop. Before discussing this, let’s take a quick look at the numbers:

Focusing on the business’ organic performance is more important than ever because Zoetis recently divested its MFA (Medicated Feed Additives) business, and FX has been all over the place. Revenue grew 9% operationally but only 1% on a reported basis because the MFA divestiture and FX shaved 400 bps each of the underlying growth.

The good news is that, to an extent, the MFA divestiture is already becoming somewhat palpable in the margin profile. The divestiture was accretive to margins (i.e., the MFA business had lower margins than average), so Zoetis has become smaller but more profitable (the sharp rise in companion as a percentage of sales was driven by the MFA divestiture, as it was a livestock business). Zoetis saw its gross margin expand 140 bps and its operating margin expand 150 bps. The MFA divestiture is not the only moving part here, but it’s definitely a tailwind.

Another thing worth pointing out is buybacks. These were the reason why EPS grew 300 bps faster than net income, and while they might seem minor in any given quarter, they surely add up over the long term. Zoetis has been scaling up buybacks as its cash flows have grown. The company repurchased $443 million of shares in Q1 and has spent almost $2 billion in buybacks over the last twelve months, corresponding to around 83% of the Free Cash Flow generated over the same period. Shares outstanding dropped 2.4% year over year. This might not seem much, but it adds up and eventually becomes a strong tailwind so long as the company keeps growing its intrinsic value. Zoetis' outstanding shares are down 5.5% over the last three years, which has helped the company grow its EPS considerably faster than its net income:

One could pinpoint two main “lowlights” from the summary table. First is the declining growth rate (I’m referring to organic here): Zoetis grew 300 bps faster in the comparable quarter (Q1 2024). This slowdown concerns the new OA pain MABs franchise, and I’ll go into this topic later on.

The other thing one could point out is cash flows. Despite growth in revenue and adjusted net income, cash flows were down slightly. Note that cash flows don’t exclude the MFA divestiture (i.e., it’s not an organic metric). To this, we have to add the fact that Zoetis’ cash flows are pretty volatile and not necessarily indicative of anything in any given quarter:

Another thing that needs context is guidance. Zoetis raised the absolute figures in revenue and adjusted net income for FY 2025, but (important “but”), this rise was caused almost entirely by the dollar's depreciation. Zoetis reports in dollars but has a significant international business, so it benefits materially from a weaker dollar.

On an operational basis (which excludes the impact of FX), the company maintained the revenue guidance, but dropped the adjusted net income guidance from a midpoint of 7% to a midpoint of 6%. This drop was caused by the topic du jour: tariffs.

Management mentioned that tariffs would have a short-term net impact of around $20 million. Zoetis exports much more to the US than it imports, so the impact primarily comes from retaliatory tariffs. However, this was inconsistent with what management said in the call. They said the main headwind came from API imports from China for its livestock business. Not very related, but API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients) are the core substance used in drug manufacturing, and API production has moved significantly to China over the last decade to save costs (this is likely what Trump doesn’t like).

The key franchises and the never-ending topic of competition

Zoetis’ key franchises (parasiticides, dermatology, and OA pain mabs) kept growing at a good pace despite their already significant size (I’ll discuss OA pain mabs separately and will focus on the other franchises here). The Simparica franchise (parasiticides) generated revenue of $367 million, growing 19% year over year. Key dermatology (mainly Apoquel) rose 10% and generated $387 million in revenue. While these numbers at this scale are pretty impressive for an animal health franchise, management could not avoid the everlasting questions around competition.

There’s no denying that the market is getting more competitive in parasiticides and dermatology, but the numbers speak for themselves. Competition is not new, and these franchises continue to grow at double-digit rates. So, why is this? I’ve discussed this before, but the competition topic is interesting for Zoetis and the animal health industry in general.

As there are many unmet needs and it’s a relatively immature industry, competition can have a positive impact on the incumbents. The main reason is that competition, instead of raising the competitive intensity, helps build awareness. The case of Simparica Trio is pretty illustrative of how this works. Competition for Simparica Trio (Zoetis’ triple-combination antiparasiticide) has been available for some time, and despite this the product continues to grow at a good pace. Why? Because entrants are accelerating the shift to triple combinations, which benefits Zoetis as the incumbent and a first-mover as mentioned in the earnings call (emphasis added):

I think what we’re really seeing is that, there’s still 40% growth in a combined flea chick heartworm space and the more people that enter, the more people get switched into combo therapies from single therapies or topicals or collars. So, we continue to see that regardless of who is entering. We have an excellent product. It’s continued to compete really well in the market.

We’re really proud of the execution on this in the US and around the globe. So, we’ve seen competition. We’re obviously going to continue to see more competition. I think what’s also something to focus on is that right now, we have a 40% share in puppies. And obviously, once one dog goes on a product, it’s, rare that it’ll switch. So I think that’s a really good indication of where that’s going in the future.

This is one of the things that makes the animal health industry interesting: the available whitespace protects against higher competition resulting in significantly higher competitive intensity. This will definitely change at some point when markets get saturated, but that point seems to be pretty far away in many of Zoetis’ markets.

Another thing worth noting relates to the distribution channel. Zoetis mentioned several interesting stats related to sales through non-vet channels:

  1. Retail grew 40% in the quarter

  2. Retail already makes up around 21% of its U.S. business

  3. Retail makes up to 40% of sales for products like Simparica and Apoquel.

I have mixed feelings here. While I completely understand that the retail channel makes it easier for people to have access to Zoetis’ products and helps drive awareness, I also feel it somewhat reduces the moat by displacing the friendly middleman (the vet). Displacing the friendly middleman also means that Zoetis is even less exposed to vet visits, but can potentially make it easier for new entrants to scale. The good news is that:

  1. Most of Zoetis' products require a prescription and therefore require at least one visit to the vet

  2. Competition is coming from the incumbents because you need the know-how and the scale to invest in R&D (meaning there are still significant barriers for new entrants).

Both of these things make me net positive on the move to the retail channel. The friendly middleman is not Zoetis’ only moat.

OA Pain MABs: what’s going on with Librela?

OA Pain MABs in general, and Librela in particular, were, in my honest opinion, the reason for the post-earnings drop. Librela kept growing at a double-digit clip globally, but despite posting a strong +17% growth in the US, management mentioned that “adoption has been more gradual than they expected.” They blamed this on two things…

  1. A deceleration in spending on chronic medications due to macro weakness (sounds like an excuse)

  2. Poor execution around awareness and education surrounding Librela.

Before considering why this might have happened, let’s look at the numbers. International and U.S. numbers are not comparable because international includes the launch of new geographies, but just looking at the U.S. data, it’s pretty obvious that Librela has decelerated significantly in the U.S.:

It may well be that the benefits of Librela are not well understood, or maybe the negative publicity that Librela has suffered lately and the label change have played a role in the reduced demand (I don’t really know what the exact reason may be, but these are some options). What is puzzling is that Librela’s development in the U.S. and Europe has been so different. While management has managed to spur growth internationally by penetrating milder cases, growth has decelerated in the U.S. before even covering all severe cases. It’s honestly very strange considering that it’s exactly the same drug and that animals are unlikely to be very different in Europe than in Germany (for example).

Regardless of the underlying reason, this deceleration is not great news for Zoetis for two reasons. First, Librela was expected to be a significant growth tailwind in the coming years. Management still believes that OA pain is a $2 to $3 billion market, but growth is significantly decelerating at around a $600 million in global sales. They still believe the category is vast and have confidence in recovering Librela’s growth…but the interesting thing is that they gave some unexpected data regarding the new long-acting OA pain mab they expect to get approval for this year. They mentioned that the new long-acting…

  1. Will require one dose every three months (compared to Librela’s 1 injection per month)

  2. Is a different molecule from Librela and therefore will not carry its name

  3. It contains 10 times fewer dose than Librela.

The first point is important because it should help with compliance; putting an injection once every three months is much better for a pet owner than every month. The second point is interesting because it should theoretically reset some of the negative publicity Librela has received; not carrying the Librela label might be good in the U.S. The third point is also very important because a lower dose probably results in significantly fewer adverse events.

All this said, management was clear in that they still trust Librela and that they will not need the commercialization of this new long-acting MAB to return to growth. Note that if the MAB gets FDA approval this year in Q4 (for example), we will not see it commercialized likely until the second half of 2026 (meaning that it’s unlikely to compensate for the lower growth in Librela). The second reason this is not great is that OA pain mabs like Librela have an accretive margin profile, and therefore, lower sales should result in a lower margin. Management also argued they would invest aggressively in awareness, so it’s actually a double-whammy for margins. This was not discussed as a reason for the reduced net income guidance, but it might well be.

What I have said here is not great news, especially because I don’t feel management has been fully transparent. Last quarter, management attributed the sequential decline of Librela sales to simply volatility related to building a new category. That commentary made sense, but things seem to have changed now. It’s not only the fact that Zoetis is building a new category; it’s also a lack of execution. Still, I remain surprised by the differences between Librela in the U.S. and international markets, and that makes me believe this is just a bump along the way. The best news for Zoetis is that it’s currently the only OA pain Mab available in the market, and the alternative (NSAIDs) is significantly worse, meaning that hiccups are unlikely to cost the company much in terms of market share and future opportunity. The long-acting MAB should, in theory, widen the gap with competitors and allow Zoetis to take a good chunk of this market (TBD if it’s as big as Zoetis claims).

I believe the problems related to Librela are unlikely to last for long, so I rate Zoetis as a buy at current prices.

data.title: Strattec Security Corporation 2025 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
