data.title: Suzano: Deleveraging Continues, End Of Downtime Should Improve Unit Economics
data.description:
Suzano (NYSE:SUZ) had an alright quarter. There were some cash cost pressures due to maintenance downtime, but the addition of the Cerrado volumes as well as some favourable supply effects on paper and pulp prices helped broker price increases in their markets to defend against the general decline that's been happening in these last months in pulp prices. Moreover, eucalyptus pulp isn't such a bad place to be either, with hardwood indices in general outperforming softwood as the long-short fibre spread closes. From an investing point of view, the fundamental story remains the deleveraging and the likely dividends that could follow on a pretty compressed price and therefore high earnings yield. There is already a decent dividend being paid, but the rapid deleveraging would permit a higher rate soon. The deleveraging is indeed progressing.

Latest Results

Let's have a rapid look at the latest results.

Key is the cash generation and net debt decline, down around 6% compared to last quarter. In the past, prior to the ramp up of the Cerrado CAPEXing, the company was able to achieve a 10% deleveraging quarterly. The price environment for pulp is less favourable now than then, but the new Cerrado volumes offset that and contribute positively to the company's overall ability to generate cash. The net debt declined by about the quantum of EBITDA, which is a reasonable proxy for cash generation at the moment, particularly with a good proportion of its facilities being brand new.

Pulp volumes are up 10% YoY on account of Cerrado, but have been down 19% sequentially due to a combination of wanting to grow inventories to more normal levels as well as downtime for maintenance.

The latter issue hit scale and consequently grew per tonne cash costs which hit the margins. While net revenue was up 22% YoY, adjusted EBITDA increased only 7%.

However, even accounting for the downtime there were higher wood costs due to needing to harvest in a larger radius (logistics factor) and more activity in slightly more distant mills (also a logistics factor). There was also a slight impact from higher caustic soda prices and natgas prices. This latter factor isn't the case for the YoY comps, where input prices were generally down. Mostly it was from the scale hit and the logistic factors hitting both the sequential and the YoY figures, both resulting in increased cash cost per tonne.

Bottom Line

Downtime of capacity in China meant some recovery in pricing has been possible, with successive price increases by the sales team being absorbed by markets for both paper and pulp. End markets remains pretty good, and the demand for hardwood pulp is growing as opposed to more pressure in softwood pulp due to the higher long-short price spread for fibre. Any sort of rallying in price is nice to see, because the macro outlook has been weighing on pulp price indices quite a bit. Prices are down close to 20% YoY.

But our conviction in the idea hasn't really changed. We expected some pressure on prices anyway, but the deleveraging path doesn't really need to be revised downwards since we'd been counting on the Cerrado volumes for a long time now, and finally they are here. The company's dividend is a relatively new phenomenon and likely not fully upsized yet at around 3% currently. NOPAT yield on the price is more than 10% at current run-rates. Even with the large debt load and considerable financial expenses eating up about half the EBIT, earnings yields are around 5% on current annualised Q1 run-rates. With the major growth CAPEX now done, we think the 5% serves as an indicative starting point for how large the dividend yield could be, with growth possible even just by a deleveraging, without needing a recovery in prices from these Q1 levels.

This is a commodity, and there is price risk on the commodity as demonstrated by its declines so far on macro, but capacity has been shrinking for the past 5 years other than from Suzano, and it'll take at least two years before any significant new capacity can possibly come online.

data.title: Transocean: Largest Customer Turning To Competition - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Transocean Ltd. or "Transocean" (NYSE:RIG) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Two weeks ago, leading offshore driller Transocean reported first quarter results slightly ahead of expectations:

Adjusted EBITDA of $244 million was above the high-end of the company's guidance provided on the Q4/2024 conference call in February:

The company attributed the outperformance to higher utilization of two harsh environment floaters, delayed out-of-service periods and the early contract commencement of the Transocean Barents in the Black Sea.

Outperformance would have been even higher without a $34 million non-cash charge resulting from the unfavorable outcome of a customer dispute.

Transocean generated $26 million in cash from operating activities and utilized $58 million for capital expenditures. The company also repaid $210 million in debt and ended the quarter with $263 million in cash and cash equivalents, $428 million in restricted cash and $6.73 billion in funded debt. Transocean's $576 million revolving credit facility remained undrawn.

As the company has failed to secure material contract awards in recent months, backlog of $7.9 billion decreased by approximately 5% on a sequential basis.

Escalating trade tensions and higher OPEC+ supply have negatively impacted oil prices over the past month thus adding a new level of uncertainty for the offshore drilling industry.

According to management on the conference call, there have been no indications of customers pulling back on long-term projects:

At this point, we do not believe that present macroeconomic uncertainty will cause delays to the programs we expect to be awarded to the industry over the next several quarters. Many of which are linked to long-term developments, independent projections and key industry metrics continue to point towards increasing offshore drilling activity across the board, with growth coming from deepwater basins. (...)

In fact, we think the second half of the year could be, very prolific in terms of long term awards.

However, competitor Noble Corporation (NE) picking up two long-term contracts with Transocean's largest customer Shell (SHEL) in the U.S. Gulf of Mexico has been raising some eyebrows among market participants as the company has a quartet of high-specification drillships scheduled to roll off contract in the region over the next couple of years:

In fact, Shell's ill-advised decision to commit to unprecedented 10-year contracts for these rigs at sky-high dayrates shortly before the offshore drilling market started to collapse in 2014 actually helped Transocean to stay afloat over the course of the downturn.

To be fair, the recent assignment of the Deepwater Thalassa to a new contract with Woodside offshore Mexico was a clear sign that Shell is not looking to extend with Transocean. The contracts announced by Noble last week will result in the requirement to also secure alternative employment for Deepwater Proteus, the second rig scheduled to roll off contract with Shell next year.

During the questions-and-answers session of the conference call, management more or less admitted to the issue but also indicated that the company was not willing to agree to the proposed terms:

Arun Jayaram

(...) I was wondering if you could or getting a few by side questions on the Shell Awards from Noble. I was wondering if you could talk about the implications from Transocean, because you do obviously have a couple of incumbent rigs working for Shell today.

Jeremy Thigpen

(...) First of all, congratulations to Noble for booking up a couple of slots there. Yes. So the way that we view that, obviously we were very involved in the process with Shell, and, we simply took the portfolio view that we weren't going to kind of go to those levels for this class of asset, but that being said, we actually think, Shell themselves have additional demand required in the Gulf of Mexico.

And we've had a long relationship with Shell, and we sincerely appreciate all of their work that has been a fabulous partnership through the years. And sure, it's going to continue.

So we think, there's still plenty of opportunities, as I kind of said in the last question, we chose to take a longer view. There's a much higher period of activity just around the corner. And the rigs that we're talking about in our fleet.

While the Noble contracts contain a weaker base dayrate, the company expects to recognize substantial performance bonuses over time which would provide for decent margins:

With the remaining two rigs (Deepwater Pontus and Deepwater Poseidon) scheduled to roll off contract in late 2027 and early 2028 respectively, there's still plenty of time for Transocean to secure new contract awards.

However, with Deepwater Proteus finishing its contract in approximately 12 months, the company will have to secure follow-on work in the not-too-distant future in order to avoid potential idle time for the rig next year.

On the conference call, management largely reiterated full year guidance:

That said, the company raised year-end liquidity expectations by $100 million to a new range of $1.45 billion to $1.55 billion due to expected cost savings and lower capital expenditures.

Please note that Transocean's liquidity projections include cash restricted for debt service requirements and the company's undrawn revolving credit facility.

Transocean also provided second quarter guidance very much in line with expectations:

Valuation-wise, the company continues to trade at a substantial premium to competitors:

Given escalating trade tensions and increasing OPEC+ output levels, I have reduced profitability expectations and lowered my price target from $3.50 to $3.00:

Please note that my valuation is based on an assigned 6x EV/EBITDA multiple as compared to 5.5x for the company's main competitors due to a number of factors:

  • Transocean remains largely protected from current market weakness due to strong near-term contract coverage
  • the company owns the world's only drillships capable of drilling and completing wells requiring 20,000 psi pressure control
  • large, high-margin backlog
  • proven ability to secure contracts at industry-leading rates

But even with the benefit of a higher EV/EBITDA multiple, Transocean remains overvalued relative to floater peers like Noble Corporation, Valaris (VAL), Odfjell Drilling (OTCPK:ODFJF) and Seadrill (SDRL) which offer substantially higher upside despite carrying much lower risk due to their strong balance sheets. Odfjell Drilling and Noble Corporation are also paying sizeable dividends.

As a result, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the shares.

Bottom Line

Transocean reported slightly better than expected first quarter results and guided Q2 largely in line with consensus estimates.

The company also tweaked full-year expectations and increased year-end liquidity projections.

While escalating trade tensions and increasing OPEC+ output levels have negatively impacted oil prices and sentiment in recent weeks, customer drilling programs have remained on track for now.

In fact, management expects contracting activity to pick up steam in the second half of they year and going into 2026.

However, as uncertainties continue to increase, I have lowered my estimates and price target for Transocean's shares.

The company continues to trade at a material premium to peers. As a result, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the stock while reducing my price target from $3.50 to $3.00.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Reason Gold Is A Short For Me
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past few weeks, SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA: GLD) has been one of the best-performing ETFs out there, with investors rushing into it as a safe haven play. This article will show why gold is unlikely to continue being in this position, making it a potential short candidate.

Gold Is Very Expensive At The Moment

Due to the most recent bull run in gold, the commodity has become increasingly expensive. Figure 1 shows the inflation-adjusted gold price. As can be seen, gold is close to an all-time high and far above the 1 standard deviation barrier. Considering that the only time gold traded at current levels was during the end of the stagflationary period in 1980, there is currently a lot priced in for gold that has not happened yet. While we might see a recession, we have not yet seen jumps in inflation due to Trump's tariffs. This means that stagflation, the historically best environment for gold, while being a possibility, is not very likely, which can be seen as gold being in a kind of bubble of a pessimistic outlook.

The same can also be seen in the gold/silver ratio shown in Figure 2. Here, gold is at one of its highest levels against silver, with the ratio only being higher in 1940 and 2020. Considering that levels of 80 have already been historically high, we might either see a rally in silver or a crash in gold, while the second option is more likely, considering the already mentioned high valuation of gold.

A last ratio to consider is that of gold to real estate, as both are considered a safe haven against inflation. As Figure 3 shows, real estate is at a low point compared to gold, which has historically only happened in 1980, just before the big decline of gold, and 2011, due to the crash in the housing market. All of this further indicates too high valuations in the gold market.

Gold Has Historically Only Performed Well During The Start Of Crashes

Some consider the future performance of gold as a function of what the market does, however, this is not necessarily the case. While it is, in my opinion, correct to assume that gold will lose some of its gains, should the market come back to normality and hit new all time highs, amongst falling foreign trade tensions, it is historically not correct that gold will continue rallying should these tensions continue, with the market falling even further. Figure 4 shows the performance of gold and SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA: SPY) from 2007 to 2009. As can be seen, gold did rise on the initial decline of the stock market. However, during the biggest decline in the S&P 500, gold suddenly fell with the index. This can be seen even better in Figure 5, showing the 2022 crash. Here, gold showed a strong rally, while the market fell (the market with the red circle). However, after the initial rally, gold suddenly moved with the market falling downward. While this has not always been the case (in 2020, gold instantly fell with the market without rallying), it is a big risk to consider and shows that the common prescription of gold being a hedge does not necessarily have to be true.

Risks

Something that, of course, always has to be considered in such a trade is the risks. The biggest one for short sellers would of course be that gold keeps rising. While I see this as highly unlikely due to the already historically high valuation, it has to be considered by investors. Because of this, it makes sense to short gold in tranches, in case prices continue to rise. The only possibility for gold to continue rising would be if governments increasingly buy gold even at prices this high to diversify away from US dollar reserves. While this is unlikely, it could still be hedged by additionally going long on EUR:USD, thereby betting on the dollar to decline.

Another option could be to bet on the spread between gold and silver due to the high comparison valuation. This would also guard against a potential rally in the commodities sector.

For better liquidity, I like GLD the best of all ETFs as it has the highest AUM.

Technicals

From a technical perspective, gold has great upside momentum at the moment and trades above its 200-day and 50-day moving averages, which generally signals short-term upside potential. For potential short entrance, however, one could consider the last high in GLD at $316.65, which could act as a resistance for further upward movements.

Conclusion

In total, gold does currently look very expensive compared to other asset classes, as well as on an inflation-adjusted basis. Furthermore, it seems like it has already priced in a far too grim outlook for the US economy. From a historical perspective, gold does not look attractive either, as it usually falls in both likely outcomes of a recession or a release of global tensions, making it an attractive short play.

data.title: Bitcoin Is A 'Price' Speculation, Gold Enduring 'Value'
data.description:
Disclaimer of bias: I have viewed Bitcoin and Crypto in general as a speculation to varying degrees from day 1. NFT? Well as soon as NFT/Crypto drones started liking and following and glad-handing me, NFTrh (@NFTRHgt) on then-Twitter simply because of three letters in the abbreviated name of my service, my Scam-O-Meter red-lined and broke. “Meme Coins”? Well, I told you my Scam-O-Meter broke so I’ll use my B/S Detector instead to take a look at the Crypto sphere vs. gold.

Crypto and especially its speculative outer edges are logical components of the massive array of investments and speculations alike that have been exponentially boosted by the decades-long bubble in monetary and fiscal policies designed to inflate asset prices at all costs. Fed and government, working the same angle from different sides of the street.

It is a bubble now indicated to be ending (if it’s not already blown) due to a ton of indicators we’ve tracked over the last year+ and in the case of the 30yr Treasury yield “Continuum”, since its big time trend break in 2022. Here is the not so pretty picture *, although details on this and other macro indicators are beyond the scope of this article.

* Unless you, like I, are a macro market nerd.

The general Crypto story that the public finds so engaging is actually quite compelling; technology and its unlimited power (unless someone trips over the plug), art/design and meme-centric creativity combine to theoretically set one free from the tyranny of the Central Banking system and the trampy debt notes of currency at its core.

Folks, the bubble is hitting its Zenith. It’s a play. Go ahead and play if you will. But understand that you are speculating, not investing (and you are not one of these 58 wallets).

Bubbles eventually burst, and the outer reaches of this one are going to simply vaporize, like the NFT scam did, while the likes of Bitcoin, when it is not being used to con the elderly out of their savings or to blackmail people with email threats of exposing the, err, personal things they do behind their screens, may have some utility. It has broken through to the mainstream corporate world in some cases.

But the sloganeering? Just hodl and get rich! That is the main pitch to newbie market players and it is a promotion for the ages. Crypto is figuratively sold to the public as currency outside the mainstream and outside the clutches of government. It is represented by its gurus (promoters) as safety and protection against government and monetary policy abuse. It is sold as monetary value of some kind, residing in server farms and organized by personal wallets and systems of transaction.

Well kids, good luck with that. Again, what happens when someone trips over the cord or worse, decides to turn out the lights? Not only is your speculation down the tubes, but the “value” that never was will be all too apparent.

I find the raging social media debates between Gold promoters and Bitcoin promoters to be especially comical. This battle of the Titans draws in all sorts of transfixed eyeballs from the multitudes who know there is something very wrong with the system that is/was. But just because both items are forms of monetary protest and revulsion, it does not mean they are even in the same universe, let alone ballpark.

  • Bitcoin: Focusing on the most viable Crypto market, BTC is data. It resides within and is exchanged within electronic devices. There are rules. There are wallets. There are electronic safeguards to ensure that your “investment” in this “money” (a contradiction in terms that any experienced investor can spot a mile away) remains safely yours until you choose to trade or spend it. Simply not true. Your legal claims to this “money” are on a server that you do not own.
  • Gold: If old time gold bugs do not even consider keeping metal in a bank safe deposit box, how can they even comprehend the idea of safe digital money on some remote server? The answer is, they can’t. I can’t. And you shouldn’t. You should understand BTC for what it is and understand gold for what it is.

Gold is a long-term marker and tracker of monetary value. It will spend years sometimes not doing what you may think it should be doing. Then, in a phase like today its price validates its promoters and their fans. The problem being, they should have felt validated no matter what the price was doing, because gold is simply an inverse reflection of the macro in which it exists. Thus, it is monetary insurance. Thus it has value.

The basis of this article is to define the difference between price speculation and value. Between a play and long-term insurance. Price-wise, BTC has drubbed gold since it began trading in 2014. The entirety of that phase (aside from a few brief and heavy corrections) was a bullish one in wider risk-on markets that were pumped by the then-ongoing policy bubble. So of course BTC has drubbed the counter-cyclical metal.

However, gold gets where it is going in the time it takes to get there. Many people are astounded at the big bull move that has blown through the 3000+ target that I personally had loaded since the Cup’s right side and Handle began to form in 2020. But it is not a bubble. It is an asset that was “counter” to bubble beneficiary assets from crypto to many hype-fueled stock sectors during the last cycle.

Today, gold, while extended on a nominal price chart, is just getting started and far from a bubble. This chart shows that gold has gone nowhere since 2021 vs. Bitcoin, and before that it only went down relative to BTC. So my points made above are not yet proven out in the relative prices. But again, price is price and value is value.

At some point the “value” aspect of gold is going to see its price start to rise vs. BTC, just as it has started (emphasis on “started”, as there’s a long way to go in this new macro) to rise vs. fellow policy-bubble recipient, the stock market (SPX).

Bottom Line

If you’re a player, understand you’re a player. You are playing Crypto along with armies of other players. You see those Meme coin wallet stats above? The 58 multi-millionaire wallets are not you. Are you the among the 764,00 losing wallets? Don’t want to be? Realize that the latest Crypto trends are easily manipulated in a tradition as old as the stock market itself. If you look around the room and you don’t know who the mark is, it is you.

If you are a holder of actual monetary value, you go long stretches (measured in years) on the outs. But if you see gold for what it is, those years are a breeze because value never goes away and insurance is always a good idea. This value gets marked down or up depending on the social/economic environment in which it exists. Right now, gold exists in an environment that his shifting counter-cyclical and post-bubble. It’s only just getting started.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Still A Buy Despite High Valuation
data.description:
Rocket Lab USA (NASDAQ:RKLB) posted its first quarter results on May 9, beating analyst estimates on the top and bottom line. The stock price declined slightly in post-market trading, which to me actually does not mean much especially since the stock price rose 29% since my last report. In that report, I discussed the importance of Rocket Lab being selected for the NSSL Phase 3 contract, enabling it to compete for launch services using its Neutron Rocket.

During the first quarter earnings call, I saw many of the positives that will play out over the mid to longer term being highlighted by management. To me, that's a positive since Rocket Lab is working on something that justifies its stock prices in the long term.

The way I view the company is that it's currently scaling up its business that gives the company some earnings growth momentum, but it's also investing in new products such as Neutron and acquiring new business for its Space Systems segment that will truly unlock new revenue streams that it can build out further.

Obviously, the earnings that the company is achieving currently are important as we do need to see some evidence that its existing business is growing nicely, but I'm particularly upbeat on the company’s longer-term prospects.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings, outlook and significant developments during the quarter. The sequence of the discussion also reflects the importance I put into what Rocket Lab presented from least important to most important. Additionally, I will update my price target for the stock.

Rocket Lab Revenues Drop Sequentially On Lower Launch ASP

First quarter revenues increased 32.1% to $122.6 million. Sequentially, revenues decreased by 7.4%. What I do find somewhat ironic is that in the prior earnings call presentation, Rocket Lab included a sequential growth figure prominently, but apparently when there's a sales decline that does not get featured. I can understand that a company would not want to prominently feature it, but I would think that it is better not to change the presentations in that way.

Gross margins increased by 100 basis points to 28.8% but declined 60 basis points to 33.4% on a non-GAAP basis. Launch revenues increased 9% year-over-year to $35.6 million and declined 16% sequentially. For companies that are in a growth phase, showing sequential development makes sense, but there's seasonality in launches with Q1 usually being soft.

Average sales price per launch declines sequentially on five launches in both quarters. So, the average revenue per launch declined from $8.5 million to $7.1 million. That's not a sign that Rocket Lab has to steeply discount launch prices to get customers. It's mostly a mix effect where multi-launch customers obtain a discount and if we have more multi-launch customers launching in a quarter compared to the other, it may decrease the revenue per launch which is what happened in Q1 2025. Year-on-year, the ASP was also lower, but this was offset by an additional launch in the quarter.

Sequentially, Space Systems sale reduced by $3 million to $87 million, marking a 3.3% decrease but a 45% increase year-on-year as the business is growing through organic and inorganic means.

During the quarter, adjusted EBITDA decreased sequentially from a $23.2 million loss to a $30 million loss while free cash flow burn increased from $23.9 million to $82.9 million driven by a receipt from a Space Development Agency program in Q4.

Rocket Lab Expects Sequential Growth In Q2

For the second quarter, Rocket Lab expects $130 million to $140 million in revenues with year-on-year growth in both segments. Sequentially, the company expects revenues to increase by 6% to 14% with margins of 34% to 36%. This is driven by ASP and launch cadence to improve throughout the quarter and higher output in the Space Systems segment and some sales shifting from Q1 into Q2. Adjusted EBITDA loss is expected to be stable sequentially or $2 million better.

Currently, the company remains loss making as investments in the development of the Neutron rocket are ongoing. So, operational results from Launch Services and Space Systems are offset by required investments for Rocket Lab to bring the Neutron rocket to the market.

What could introduce some softness in the stock price is the fact that analysts are expecting revenues of $137.5 million, which is above the mid-point of the guidance that management provided. However, overall I'm not too concerned given the fact that the company has only missed revenue estimates three times in the past 15 quarters.

Rocket Lab Sees Progress On Business Developments

During the first quarter, Rocket Lab was selected to compete on launch services for the NSSL Phase 3 contract using the Neutron rocket. Previously, I already attested that this is a major sign of confidence in Rocket Launch from the Department of Defense and the value Rocket Lab offers to the DoD to bring defense payloads into orbit. Management also underscored the vote of confidence during the call:

Our selection to it is a huge vote of confidence by the Pentagon and Neutron and affirms us as one of the most capable American launch providers. We are also the only publicly traded company to ever onboard NSSL. Once we are clear of Neutron's first launch, we'll be bidding for task orders under the Phase 3 Lane 1 program which has a total value of $5.6 billion and an ordering period through to June 2029. We've already completed a kickoff meeting with the full contingent of future mission partners including the U.S. Space Force, Assured Access to Space, NRO, Office of Space Launch and other stakeholders from across the government. This was part of a $5 million task order for a mission assurance showcase that came with Neutron selection.

Besides Neutron launch capabilities expecting to go live enabling revenue streams from a different launch payload bracket, Rocket Lab is also seeing increased momentum for its HASTE vehicle for hypersonic test launch capability. The company is already part of the EWAAC (Enterprise-Wide Agile Acquisition Contract) and MACH-TB programs and the company has also been selected by the UK Ministry of Defense for the $1.3 billion framework for hypsonic capabilities.

In March, I discussed the acquisition of Mynaric, which in my view guarantees Rocket Lab access to the terminals it has ordered, bolsters its space products and customer portfolio and could eventually be leveraged for Rocket Lab’s own constellation development.

Unsurprisingly, the company also confirmed my view on the importance on the meaning of the acquisition beyond “just an inorganic growth opportunity”:

Now by bringing them in-house, the terminals in-house we will add a new element to our spacecraft supply chain that improves our product line and strengthens our position in commercial, national security and defense contracts.

Mynaric terminals are already being supplied for a half a billion dollar contract with the Space Development Agency along with many other companies, making this even more of a logical integration. We've proven across all of our acquisitions to date that we can take a highly sought after product, scale it and make it available in high volume. It's our full intention to do the same here again by expanding into Europe and to bring Mynaric terminals to the world and potentially for our own Constellation too. So I'm excited about the potential of this deal and we'll be sure to keep you updated on its progress.

During the quarter, Rocket Lab completed the Stage Flight Hardware and Structures Qualification. So, the second stage will be going through final assembly and be shipped for testing with the engine in the next few months. Stage 1 is close to completion as well which would provide a major milestone completion for the rocket.

The company currently is planning for a launch in the second half of the year. Based on how many items remain to be completed, there remains some risk. Previously the launch was set for mid 2025 and that has now slipped into the second half of 2025. I would not be surprised if a second half launch target indicates a launch very late in 2025. From there, if there's any slip in the schedule, you would be looking at a launch in 2026.

So, there's significant progress, but a lot of work remains to be completed.

Rocket Lab Remains A Buy On Mid-Term Growth Expectations

Rocket Lab still is not a stock that is extremely cheap. I have modeled a price target assessment based on the price-to-sales method and assumed that the acquisition of Mynaric will be settled through shares being issued. For 2025, the stock is definitely overvalued and against FY26 sales, the stock is overvalued by around 4%. Allowing a stock to trade year ahead of its results makes sense to me. So, the current price is actually a fair one and it also is close to the averaged price targets based on the median and minimum price-to-sales for 2027 and in line with the minimum for 2028. That's where I believe the value of the stock is comfortable and those levels are only slightly below the current levels.

However, if we look at where the price could potentially be heading in the years ahead, we see 32% upside for 2027 and stock prices doubling for 2028 with the median and minimum implied stock price targets offering 48% upside, which is my bullish case. So, if you buy now you're definitely not buying at a discount but we also see an opportunity for longer-term upside which I believe continues to support a buy thesis granted you are not hesitant to pay a premium because given the business prospects it is possible that the company will continue to trade at a premium in which case you may miss upside waiting for a more compelling entry point.

The only ways in which I believe we could see a significant reduction in the share prices is in a marketwide sell off or if Neutron rocket development experiences significant delays. The third risk would be losing contracts, most notably the SDA Tranche 2 for which Rocket Lab is under contract to provide part of the satellites, as we do see that the DoD may potentially change course in an effort to cut costs. At the same time, we do see that President Donald Trump is eyeing a $1 trillion security budget. So, there could be a pressure but measured against the opportunities and the market demand commercial and from government customers (not just US Government) the opportunities far outweigh the headwinds.

Conclusion: Rocket Lab Remains My Favorite End-to-End Space Company

The Rocket Lab valuation remains stretched and that may result in softer share prices if investors get too accustomed to the achievements of Rocket Lab and price in any potential bad news in more aggressively.

However, I do like the growth in the business, the expansion of the launch capabilities and the acquisition of companies bolstering the product portfolio that can be used to get one step closer to provide complete end-to-end opportunities from satellite design to launch and orbit injection. Furthermore, we see that the DoD is valuing Rocket Lab as a commercial partner giving it a spot to compete for hypersonics testing and compete for defense payload deliveries.

I believe that the stock remains a strong buy on its bright prospects despite its high valuation.
